edges.description: Reduced or absent nesprin-1 impairs assembly and function of the LINC complex at the nuclear envelope.
Disrupted nuclear-cytoskeletal coupling in cerebellar neurons leads to cerebellar degeneration manifesting as diffuse cerebellar atrophy.
The broader SYNE1 deficiency spectrum includes upper motor neuron signs beyond the pure cerebellar phenotype.
Cerebellar-plus SYNE1 deficiency can include lower motor neuron involvement outside the classic pure cerebellar presentation.
Diffuse cerebellar atrophy is the imaging correlate of the cerebellar neurodegenerative process.
Cerebellar degeneration produces the progressive cerebellar ataxia syndrome.
Cerebellar degeneration manifests clinically as gait ataxia.
Cerebellar degeneration impairs appendicular coordination, producing limb ataxia.
Cerebellar degeneration causes the dysarthria component of the syndrome.
Cerebellar degeneration causes dysmetria as part of the pure cerebellar syndrome.
Cerebellar degeneration affects ocular motor control, including smooth pursuit.
Cerebellar ocular motor involvement includes slow saccadic eye movements.
Cerebellar ocular motor involvement includes gaze-evoked nystagmus.
Cerebellar involvement can extend to cerebellar cognitive and affective syndrome.

nodes.description: Impaired smooth pursuit eye movements, an ocular motor abnormality of the cerebellar syndrome.

evidence.snippet: abnormalities in ocular saccades and smooth pursuit
The diagnosis of SYNE1 deficiency is established in a proband with suggestive findings and biallelic SYNE1 pathogenic variants identified by molecular genetic testing.

evidence.reference_title: Autosomal Recessive Cerebellar Ataxia Type 1: Phenotypic and Genetic Correlation in a Cohort of Chinese Patients with SYNE1 Variants.
evidence.snippet: We identified eight truncating variants and two missense variants spreading throughout the SYNE1 gene from six unrelated families

nodes.description: Cerebellar neuronal vulnerability, supported by the strong cerebellar expression of SYNE1, produces diffuse cerebellar atrophy without cortical, brainstem, or white-matter involvement in the classic founder phenotype, and underlies the progressive cerebellar ataxia, dysarthria, and dysmetria.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and genetic study of autosomal recessive cerebellar ataxia type 1.
evidence.snippet: slow progression and moderate disability, significant dysarthria, mild oculomotor abnormalities, occasional brisk reflexes in the lower extremities, normal nerve conduction studies, and diffuse cerebellar atrophy on imaging

nodes.description: Progressive gait and limb ataxia is the cardinal and near-universal feature, typically with adult onset (mean ~31.6 years) in the founder phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and genetic study of autosomal recessive cerebellar ataxia type 1.
evidence.snippet: ARCA-1 is a cerebellar syndrome characterized by recessive transmission, middle-age onset (mean, 31.60; range, 17-46 years), slow progression and moderate disability

nodes.description: Diffuse cerebellar atrophy on CT/MRI is a consistent imaging finding, without cortical, brainstem, or white-matter involvement in the classic phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and genetic study of autosomal recessive cerebellar ataxia type 1.
evidence.snippet: normal nerve conduction studies, and diffuse cerebellar atrophy on imaging

nodes.description: Cognitive impairment and the cerebellar cognitive and affective syndrome (deficits in attention, executive function, verbal working memory, visuospatial skills) occur in many patients; intellectual disability is seen in childhood-onset multisystem disease.

evidence.snippet: Most individuals develop features of the cerebellar cognitive and affective syndrome (i.e., significant deficits in attention, executive functioning, verbal working memory, and visuospatial/visuoconstructional skills).

nodes.description: Nesprin-1 participates in the LINC complex spanning the nuclear envelope (SUN-KASH bridging), physically connecting the cytoskeleton to nuclear components and mediating nuclear positioning, cell migration, and mechanotransduction. Loss of nesprin-1 disrupts this coupling. The LINC complex is essential to nervous-system development, and LINC-gene mutations are implicated in neurodegenerative disease.

evidence.reference_title: Diverse Roles of the LINC Complex in Cellular Function and Disease in the Nervous System.
evidence.snippet: The linker of nucleoskeleton and cytoskeleton (LINC) complex, which spans the nuclear envelope, physically connects nuclear components to the cytoskeleton and plays a pivotal role in various cellular processes, including nuclear positioning, cell migration, and chromosomal configuration.

nodes.description: Cerebellar (scanning) dysarthria, present in essentially all patients in the founder cohort.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and genetic study of autosomal recessive cerebellar ataxia type 1.
evidence.snippet: slow progression and moderate disability, significant dysarthria

nodes.description: Impaired coordination of movement amplitude, part of the cerebellar syndrome.

evidence.snippet: some individuals have a pure cerebellar syndrome (i.e., cerebellar ataxia, dysarthria, dysmetria, abnormalities in ocular saccades and smooth pursuit)

nodes.description: Unsteady, ataxic gait, frequently the presenting symptom.

evidence.reference_title: Eye-tracking-aided characterization of saccades and antisaccades in SYNE1 ataxia patients: a pilot study.
evidence.snippet: SYNE1 ataxia is an autosomal recessive hereditary condition, the main characteristic features of which are gait and limb ataxia and cerebellar dysarthria.

evidence.reference_title: Eye-tracking-aided characterization of saccades and antisaccades in SYNE1 ataxia patients: a pilot study.
evidence.snippet: the main characteristic features of which are gait and limb ataxia and cerebellar dysarthria

nodes.description: Lower motor neuron dysfunction and motor neuron disease features occur in a subset of patients, particularly in non-founder cohorts; variants associated with motor neuron involvement tend to cluster in the C-terminal region.

evidence.reference_title: Autosomal Recessive Cerebellar Ataxia Type 1: Phenotypic and Genetic Correlation in a Cohort of Chinese Patients with SYNE1 Variants.
evidence.snippet: four patients exhibited non-cerebellar phenotypes, including motor neuron symptoms, cognitive impairment, or mental retardation

nodes.description: Gaze-evoked nystagmus reported as an ocular motor abnormality in SYNE1 ataxia patients.

evidence.reference_title: Eye-tracking-aided characterization of saccades and antisaccades in SYNE1 ataxia patients: a pilot study.
evidence.snippet: only the occurrence of gaze-evoked nystagmus, slowing of saccades, broken up smooth pursuits, strabismus and square-wave jerks were reported

nodes.description: ARCA1 is caused by biallelic SYNE1 variants, most commonly truncating loss-of- function alleles, that reduce or abolish nesprin-1. This establishes the primary molecular lesion. SYNE1 is among the largest human genes, and the spectrum of pathogenic variants includes nonsense, frameshift, splice, missense, and even large intragenic deletions detectable only by CNV-sensitive methods.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and genetic study of autosomal recessive cerebellar ataxia type 1.
evidence.snippet: We identified a cluster of French-Canadian families with a new recessive ataxia of relatively pure cerebellar type caused by mutations in SYNE1.

evidence.reference_title: A Case Report of SYNE1 Deficiency-Mimicking Mitochondrial Disease and the Value of Pangenomic Investigations.
evidence.snippet: compound heterozygous for a known nonsense variant (c.13258C>T, p.(Arg4420Ter)), and a large intragenic deletion that was predicted to result in a loss of function

nodes.description: Abnormal (slow and/or hypometric) saccadic eye movements documented by eye tracking in SYNE1 ataxia patients.

evidence.reference_title: Eye-tracking-aided characterization of saccades and antisaccades in SYNE1 ataxia patients: a pilot study.
evidence.snippet: The eye tracking assessment revealed hypometric saccades in the longer amplitude (18.4°) saccadic paradigm in all SYNE1 patients, whereas 2 out of 3 SYNE1 subjects performed slow saccades as well.

nodes.description: A subset of SYNE1-deficient patients show upper motor neuron signs (spasticity, hyperreflexia, Babinski sign), part of the broader cerebellar-plus spectrum.

evidence.snippet: many also have upper motor neuron dysfunction (spasticity, hyperreflexia, Babinski sign) and/or lower motor neuron dysfunction (amyotrophy, reduced reflexes, fasciculations)
